title: Vegetable Cooking Methods
content:
COOKING METHODS

Boiling: Rapid cooking in boiling salted water until tender. For mild and strong flavored vegetables, cook rapidly in small quantity water; keep covered; save cooking water for soups/sauces. Leave as whole as possible.

Steaming: In steamer over boiling water. Excellent for most vegetables; retains nutrients and color.

Baking: Thoroughly washed, blemishes removed. Usually baked in shell/skin or cut before cooking.

Sautéing: In small amount of fat over high heat. Pan and fat must be hot before adding vegetables.

Deep-frying: Slice or cut suitable shapes; dry well; coat if desired; fry 350-375°F until golden.

Pressure-cooking: Saves time and nutrients. Follow manufacturer directions. For steaming rack method: allow minute or two longer than pressure-cooked times.

Stir-frying/Skillet-panning: High heat with rapid stirring. Vegetables uniformly sliced; tougher ones first. Steam covered at finish 3-8 minutes until tender-crisp.

RETAINING NUTRIENTS & COLOR

Cook only until tender; transfer at once from cook to consumer. Do not use baking soda (destroys nutrients, makes mushy). Color lost in hard water—cook "à blanc" (with lemon juice/flour) for cabbage family. Salt at beginning of cooking (¼ teaspoon per cup water). Add pinch of sugar to older vegetables that have lost natural sugars.

High nutrient loss in mashing/pureeing (¼-½ vitamin C lost). Utilize all cooking waters unless bitter/off-flavored.

SERVING

Toss in butter (1-2 teaspoons per cup cooked vegetables) for simple preparation. Add citrus juices, zests, herbs, curry, mustard, chili, horseradish, cheese. For creamed: ½-¾ cup medium white sauce per cup of vegetable. For casseroles: enough sauce to just cover vegetables; may finish Au Gratin.
